analysis.quote: With Mansoor Delane off the board right before this pick, Miami pivots to McCoy and still walks away with a tone setting corner. He brings the confidence and competitive edge this defense leans on, challenging throws and playing the ball like it belongs to him. In a division loaded with speed, his foot quickness and instincts matter right away. Health is the swing factor, but if he checks out, this feels like a plug and play starter with real turnover upside.

Stowers at 43 adds a smooth, reliable target who fits perfectly in a timing based passing attack. He understands spacing, works open naturally, and gives the quarterback stress free completions that keep drives alive. He is tougher after the catch than expected, which adds hidden yardage. If he proves sturdy enough as an in line blocker, Miami suddenly has a tight end who rarely needs to leave the field.

Rodriguez injects juice into the linebacker room. He flies to the football, closes fast, and plays with an edge that shows up on early downs and pressure looks. In a conference full of motion and misdirection, discipline will be key, but the athletic tools are there. If he handles space responsibilities, this could be a three down defender who pops on Sundays.

Dunker brings a little nastiness to the offensive line, and that is not a bad thing. He profiles as a rugged right tackle or swing option who wants to move people in the run game. Speed off the edge will test him early, but the strength and temperament are easy bets. In this range, Miami gets a developmental lineman with starter upside and real edge.

Rutledge feels like a culture fit inside. He plays with leverage, toughness, and a steady snap to snap demeanor that stabilizes the interior. In the run game he can create movement, and he moves well enough to function in a varied scheme. Clean up the pass protection details and Miami could have a long term starter in the middle rounds.

Clark may not steal headlines, but he settles a secondary. He communicates, tackles with purpose, and keeps things organized on the back end. For a defense that mixes looks, having a safety you can trust matters. He projects as a reliable rotational piece with special teams value and spot start capability.

This is the classic Day 3 traits swing. Allar looks the part and the arm talent can light up a practice field in a hurry. When he is decisive, the ball jumps, and you see why teams stay intrigued. The consistency has to catch up, but at this price the upside behind the starter makes sense.

At 227, this is pure speed and upside. A 4.27 athlete with receiver background ball skills is hard to ignore late in the draft. Early impact likely comes on special teams and sub packages, but the recovery range is real. If the technique develops, Miami could quietly uncover a playmaker at the end of the board.
